title: Flexible Input
chapterTopics: String methods, input normalization, method chaining

overview.body:
You felt the friction. For three weeks, every yes/no prompt in your game required four comparisons: answer == 'y' or answer == 'Y' or answer == 'n' or answer == 'N'. Every menu choice needed two branches just to handle case. Today that ends.

Chapter 8 introduces string methods: small but powerful tools that transform how you handle user input. .upper(), .lower(), and .strip() let you normalize what the player types before you ever compare it. You will refactor function_library.py with two new helper functions, update get_yes_no(), and apply consistent input cleaning across your entire game loop. When you are done, your code will be shorter, more readable, and far more forgiving of sloppy typing.

mentorQuote.quote: Three weeks ago, get_yes_no() had to handle 'y' AND 'Y' AND 'n' AND 'N'. That is four comparisons to do one job. .upper() collapses all of that into one. When a function shrinks and still does everything it did before, that is a sign you found the right tool.

objectives: Apply .upper() and .strip() to normalize user input before comparison
Chain string methods to clean input in a single expression
Refactor get_yes_no() using .upper() to eliminate redundant OR conditions
Add clean_input() and clean_input_lower() to function_library.py
Update all user input in lab-4-2.py to use the new helper functions
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 8: Programs 8-1 (iterating over strings), 8-5 (string testing methods), and 8-9 and 8-10 (splitting strings). String modification methods (.upper(), .lower(), and .strip()) are covered in the Chapter 8 methods reference table.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapters 8 and 9
programRequirements: function_library.py contains clean_input() and clean_input_lower() functions
get_yes_no() uses .upper() with no OR chains comparing both uppercase and lowercase variants
lab-4-2.py applies .strip() or clean_input() to every user input before comparison
All functions have docstrings; file has top-of-file documentation header
overview.additionalFilesNote: This lab updates both lab-4-2.py and function_library.py. Submit both files. CodeGrade will test your library functions independently.
overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-2.py and function_library.py this week.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep both files. Module 4.3 adds sets and a trivia gate on top of the input handling you build here.

rows.task: Add clean_input() and clean_input_lower() to function_library.py
Replace OR chains with .strip().upper() (the pain-before-magic payoff)

rows.checkpoint: CP3: Clean All Input in lab-4-2.py
rows.task: Apply input normalization across the entire game loop
Test edge cases, verify CodeGrade, clean up and submit

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding to function_library.py. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes in lab-4-2.py from your 4.1 code. Everything else carries forward unchanged.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS (lab-4-2.py) ----
CITY_DISTANCES = { ... }              (from 4.1, unchanged)
CITY_INFO      = { ... }              (from 4.1, unchanged)
STARTING_CITY  = 'San Antonio'        (unchanged)

# ---- FUNCTION LIBRARY (function_library.py) ----
clean_input(prompt)                   [NEW] strip + upper
clean_input_lower(prompt)             [NEW] strip + lower
get_yes_no(prompt)                    [REPLACED] now uses .upper()
get_valid_int(prompt, min, max)       (unchanged)
flip_coin()                           (unchanged)
roll_d20()                            (unchanged)
display_numbered_list(items, title)   (unchanged)
save_list_to_file(filename, data)     (unchanged)
load_list_from_file(filename)         (unchanged)
file_exists(filename)                 (unchanged)

# ---- MAIN GAME (lab-4-2.py) ----
GAME LOOP:
│
│── Player name input         .strip()           [REPLACED]
│── Main menu choice          .strip().upper()   [REPLACED]
│
│   DESTINATION SELECTION:
│   │── Destination input     .strip().upper()   [REPLACED]
│   │── Hazard prompt        get_yes_no()        (now cleaner)
│   │
│   TRAVEL LOOP:
│   │── All prompts normalized before comparison [REPLACED]
│
│── Play again?               get_yes_no()       (now cleaner)
│
All user input normalized before any comparison.
No more 'y' vs 'Y' failures.

content.body: Open function_library.py from your 4.1 submission. You will add two new functions at the bottom of the STRING FUNCTIONS section. These functions wrap Python's built-in string methods so the rest of your code can call one clean function instead of chaining methods inline every time.

items: Add a # STRING FUNCTIONS (Added in 4.2) comment block at the bottom of your function library to separate the new additions.

Write a function named clean_input that accepts one parameter: prompt. It should get input from the user, strip leading and trailing whitespace, convert the result to uppercase, and return that cleaned string. Include a docstring that describes what it does, the parameter, and the return value.
Write a second function named clean_input_lower that works the same way but converts to lowercase instead of uppercase. It should also accept a prompt parameter and include a docstring.
In your Python shell, test both functions manually: call clean_input('Test: ') and type   hello   with spaces. Verify the return value is 'HELLO' with no spaces.
Repeat the test with clean_input_lower and verify the return value is 'hello'.

references.description: Shows how individual characters behave when tested with ==, which is the same logic that makes case comparison fragile without normalization.
Demonstrates string testing methods (.isdigit(), .isalpha(), .isupper(), and .islower()) , useful for input validation beyond case normalization.

content.body: Method chaining is the key idea here. When you write input(prompt).strip().upper(), Python executes left to right: input() gets a string from the user, .strip() removes whitespace from both ends of that string, and .upper() converts the result to uppercase. Each method returns a new string, and the next method is called on that result. Strings are immutable in Python, so nothing is modified in place. You are building a pipeline.

rows: input is a function; the prompt goes inside the parentheses
Function runs but the caller gets None back
Make sure the last expression in your function is a return statement
You get a reference to the method object, not the result
Writing `text = text.strip()` when you just want the cleaned value once
If you only use it once, chain directly: `input(prompt).strip().upper()`

content.title: CP1 Complete
items: function_library.py has a STRING FUNCTIONS section with clean_input() and clean_input_lower()
Both functions have docstrings with parameter and return descriptions
Manual testing confirms clean_input('Test: ') with '  hello  ' returns 'HELLO'
Manual testing confirms clean_input_lower('Test: ') with '  HELLO  ' returns 'hello'

content.body: This is the payoff checkpoint. Open function_library.py and find your existing get_yes_no() function. You are going to rewrite it using .strip().upper() and watch it get shorter, cleaner, and more capable all at once.
Here is what your current function has to do to handle both cases:
After normalizing with .upper(), the logic collapses. There are only two valid uppercase answers to check, and the return statement becomes a single comparison.

items: Find get_yes_no() in your function library. Read through its current logic and count how many comparisons it makes.
Rewrite the first input() call inside the function so it chains .strip() and .upper() before assigning to answer.
Update the while loop condition. After normalizing, you only need to compare against 'Y' and 'N'. No need to check both cases.
Update the input() call inside the loop to also use .strip().upper().
Simplify the return statement. Hint: after normalization, returning whether answer equals 'Y' is all you need. No if/else required.
Update the docstring to note that the function now uses .upper() for case-insensitive input handling.
Import your library in a Python shell: import function_library
Call function_library.get_yes_no('Continue? (y/n): ') and type lowercase y. Verify it returns True.
Call it again and type uppercase Y. Verify it also returns True.
Call it again and type   y   with leading and trailing spaces. Verify it still returns True.
Verify that n, N, and  n  all return False.
Verify that typing something invalid (like yes) triggers the error message and re-prompts.

content.quote: Count the comparisons in your old get_yes_no(), then count the comparisons in the new one. If that number went down and the function still works, you did it right. Fewer moving parts means fewer places to break.

content.title: CP2 Complete
items: get_yes_no() no longer uses OR chains to handle both 'y' and 'Y'
Both the initial input and the loop's re-prompt use .strip().upper()
The return statement is a single comparison with no if/else needed
Docstring updated to describe the .upper() normalization
Manual testing confirms 'y', 'Y', and '  y  ' all return True

content.body: Open lab-4-2.py from your 4.1 submission and save a copy as lab-4-2.py. Work through the entire file and apply input normalization everywhere a player types something. This is a refactor pass, not glamorous work. But it is the kind of pass that separates code that works for the developer from code that works for the player.

items: Start at the top of your game loop. Find every input() call that reads a player choice: destination selection, menu options, hazard responses, and any other prompt that leads to a comparison.

For any input that gets compared against letter strings (like 'A', 'B', 'Y', 'N'), replace the bare input(prompt) call with input(prompt).strip().upper(). You may also use your new clean_input(prompt) function from the library. Both are acceptable.
For the player's name input (which is not compared against a fixed set of values), apply only .strip(). Do not convert names to uppercase.
Find every while loop condition that currently checks both lowercase and uppercase (like answer != 'a' and answer != 'A') and simplify it to check only the uppercase version.
Find every if/elif chain that currently checks both cases (like if choice == 'a' or choice == 'A':) and simplify each condition to check only the uppercase version.

After all replacements, do a quick scan for any remaining lowercase letter literals in conditions. If you see == 'a', == 'b', or similar, those are candidates for cleanup. Verify they still make sense after normalization.

content.title: Use VS Code's Find to Catch Everything
content.body: In VS Code, use Ctrl+F (or Cmd+F on Mac) and search for input( to jump to every input call in your file. Work through them one at a time. It takes five minutes and ensures you do not miss any.

content.quote: I typed ' y ' with spaces on both sides. Your program crashed. Then I typed 'Y' and it worked fine. Those should behave identically. If your input handling works for some inputs but not others, it does not work. It just works for you.

items: Run your game from the terminal. At the first yes/no prompt, type a lowercase letter. Verify it is accepted..
At the first menu choice prompt, type a lowercase letter. Verify it selects the correct option..
At any prompt, type a valid answer with a leading space (like  a). Verify it still works..
Try typing all-caps where the game expects a single letter. Verify it works..
Play through at least one complete trip to confirm the game runs without errors before moving to CP4.

content.title: CP3 Complete
items: Every player choice input in lab-4-2.py uses .strip() at minimum
Every input compared against letter constants uses .strip().upper() or clean_input()
Player name input uses .strip() only; name is not uppercased
All if/elif conditions check only uppercase literals; no more 'a' or 'A' patterns
The game plays through a complete trip without input errors

content.body: Before submitting, run your game through the following test scenarios. Each one targets a specific thing the refactor was supposed to fix.

rows: Name stored without the leading space

items: Run each test case from the table above. Note any failures. If something does not behave as expected, trace back to the input call that handles that prompt.
Verify your top-of-file documentation header is present in both lab-4-2.py and function_library.py. Required fields: Name, abc123, Section, and a one-sentence description of what the file does.
Verify every function in both files has a docstring. Quick check: open each file and scan for def . Every one should be followed by a docstring on the next indented line.
Run Flake8 on both files and fix any warnings before submitting.

Submit both lab-4-2.py and function_library.py to CodeGrade. Review the automated feedback. If any correctness tests fail, read the error output carefully and trace it back to the specific input that is not being cleaned.

content.title: CodeGrade Tests Your Library Separately
content.body: CodeGrade imports your function_library.py directly and calls clean_input() and get_yes_no() in isolation. Make sure those functions work correctly on their own, not just when the full game is running.

content.title: Lab 4.2 Complete
items: Both lab-4-2.py and function_library.py submitted to CodeGrade
All test cases from the table above pass
Documentation headers present in both files
All functions have docstrings
Flake8 returns no warnings on either file

beforeYouSubmit: function_library.py contains clean_input() and clean_input_lower() with docstrings
get_yes_no() uses .strip().upper() with no OR chains comparing both 'y' and 'Y'
Every player input in lab-4-2.py uses .strip() at minimum before comparison
Player name uses .strip() only, not converted to uppercase
All if/elif and while conditions compare against uppercase literals only
Top-of-file documentation header in both files: Name, abc123, Section, Description
All functions in both files have docstrings
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: You may use .upper(), .lower(), .strip(), .lstrip(), .rstrip(), .isdigit(), .isalpha(), method chaining, and all dictionary techniques from Chapter 9 that you used in 4.1. Do NOT use list comprehensions, try/except blocks, classes, or inheritance.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (all techniques from Chapters 2–8 fully permitted; Chapter 9 dictionaries as introduced in 4.1)
specific: Dictionary access and methods from 4.1: key access, .get(), .keys(), .values(), .items()
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, try/except, sets (coming in 4.3), classes, inheritance

warnings: Do not use try/except for input validation in this lab. That pattern is out of scope. Use while loops and the string testing methods from Chapter 8.
CodeGrade imports function_library.py directly. A syntax error in your library file will cause all library tests to fail, even if your game runs fine.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files. Module 4.3 adds a sets-based unique city tracker and a trivia gate. both build on the cleaned-up input handling you just wrote.

needHelp.chapterResource: Chapter 8 (More About Strings) in Gaddis covers string methods starting in Section 8.3. The methods reference table at the end of your Chapter 8 BookEx is a quick lookup for .upper(), .lower(), .strip(), and related methods.
needHelp.discordNote: Post your question in the course Discord. Include which checkpoint you are on and what behavior you expected versus what actually happened.
